Name: JARS-Longitudinal
LongName: JARS-Quant Table 4: Reporting Standards for Longitudinal Studies
Description: The JARS-Quant longitudinal table adds reporting expectations specific to studies that observe the same unit on the same response variables across multiple occasions.
Aim: To improve the reporting of longitudinal studies by specifying expectations for wave-by-wave sample description, attrition, contextual changes, timing, missing data, analytic assumptions, and overlap with prior publications.

Scope.Coverage: Psychology and related behavioral or educational studies that repeatedly observe the same unit over time or across prespecified occasions.
Scope.Focus: Reporting of sample characteristics, recruitment and retention, attrition, contextual changes over time, timing of waves, missing-data handling, longitudinal analytic assumptions, and prior publication overlap.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not intended for single-session within-subject designs, multivariate-outcome designs without repeated measurement of the same attribute, or time-series experiments with distinct standards. This module is used together with the relevant JARS-Quant base design.

primary.description: Appelbaum M, Cooper H, Kline RB, Mayo-Wilson E, Nezu AM, Rao SM. Journal Article Reporting Standards for Quantitative Research in Psychology: The APA Publications and Communications Board Task Force Report. American Psychologist. 2018. PMID: 29345484.

items.item_name: Describe longitudinal sample characteristics and recruitment unit
items.item_question: Does the manuscript report the sampling or reporting unit and the key sample characteristics relevant to longitudinal interpretation?
items.item_question_background: Longitudinal studies require a clear statement of the unit being followed and enough demographic and contextual information to understand who is retained across waves.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract the reporting unit and sample characteristics such as N per group, age, sex, ethnicity, socioeconomic status, family or geographic context, when applicable.

source_page_refs.quote: Describe reporting (sampling or randomization) unit ... N per group, age, and sex distribution ... Country, region, city, and geographic characteristics.

items.item_question: Does the manuscript report attrition at each wave and any differential attrition across major sociodemographic groups or experimental conditions?
items.item_question_background: Attrition patterns can drive bias in longitudinal inference and must be described wave by wave rather than only as a final sample count.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract attrition at each wave, reasons for attrition, and any differential attrition by key characteristics or conditions.

source_page_refs.quote: Report attrition at each wave, breaking down reasons for attrition. Report any differential attrition by major sociodemographic and experimental condition.

items.item_name: Describe contextual changes over the life of the study
items.item_question: Does the manuscript report important contextual changes for participants or units as the study progressed?
items.item_question_background: Major contextual shifts can alter the interpretation of longitudinal change, especially in long-running developmental, educational, or social studies.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract any reported contextual changes such as school closures, economic changes, social shifts, or major life events that matter for interpreting later waves.

source_page_refs.quote: Report any contextual changes for participants (units) as the study progressed.

items.item_question: Does the manuscript specify the independent and dependent variables at each wave and the years or occasions of data collection?
items.item_question_background: Longitudinal interpretation depends on knowing what was measured when, and whether constructs or measurement schedules changed over time.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract the independent and dependent variables measured at each wave and the years or prespecified occasions when data were collected.

source_page_refs.quote: Specify independent variables and dependent variables at each wave of data collection. Report the years in which each wave of the data collection occurred.

items.item_name: Report missing data handling and analytic assumptions
items.item_question: Does the manuscript report the amount of missing data, how it was handled analytically, and which analytic approaches and assumptions were used?
items.item_question_background: Longitudinal designs nearly always encounter missingness and modeling decisions that strongly affect estimates of change over time.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract the extent of missing data, the analytic handling of missingness, the longitudinal analytic approach, and any stated assumptions.

source_page_refs.quote: Report the amount of missing data and how issues of missing data where handled analytically. Specify analytic approaches utilized and assumptions made in performing these analyses.
source_page_refs.locator: Table 4 missing data and analysis rows

items.item_question: Does the manuscript explain where portions of the data were previously published and the degree of overlap with the current report?
items.item_question_background: Longitudinal datasets are often reused across multiple reports, so transparent disclosure of overlap is essential.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract any statement about prior publications from the same longitudinal dataset and the extent of overlap with the present manuscript.

source_page_refs.quote: Provide information on where any portions of the data have been previously published and the degree of overlap with current report.

EvidenceExamplesPositive: A developmental psychology paper reports wave-by-wave attrition, contextual changes, years of data collection, and overlap with earlier publications.
A longitudinal educational study explains missing-data handling and analytic assumptions alongside repeated observations of the same participants.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A single-session within-subject experiment is not a JARS-Longitudinal match.
A meta-analysis with no repeated participant observations should not map to this module.
Matching.Explain: Use this module for psychology manuscripts that repeatedly observe the same unit across multiple occasions and need longitudinal-specific reporting.
